section.title: Revive Us Again
section.sectionRationale: A communal prayer of the sons of Korah near the close of Book III, this psalm looks back to a past forgiveness and restoration, then pleads for a fresh revival, and closes with a vision of steadfast love, faithfulness, righteousness, and peace meeting together in the land, a hope that anticipates the mercy and justice that meet fully at the cross.
section.thesis: Israel calls on the God who once forgave and restored her to revive her again, trusting that His steadfast love and faithfulness, His righteousness and peace, will meet and kiss, a hope fulfilled when Christ, in whom mercy and truth embrace, goes before His people to prepare the way.
section.frameNote: Four movements: remembering God's past forgiveness and restraint of wrath, verses 1-3; pleading for renewed favor and revival, verses 4-7; listening for the LORD's word of peace, verses 8-9; and a vision of loving devotion, faithfulness, righteousness, and peace meeting to bless the land, verses 10-13.

verses.text: You showed favor to Your land, O LORD; You restored Jacob from captivity.
You forgave the iniquity of Your people; You covered all their sin. Selah
You withheld all Your fury; You turned from Your burning anger.
Restore us, O God of our salvation, and put away Your displeasure toward us.
Will You be angry with us forever? Will You draw out Your anger to all generations?
Will You not {{chayah|revive}} us again, that Your people may rejoice in You?
Show us Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}, O LORD, and grant us Your salvation.
I will listen to what God the LORD will say; for He will surely speak peace to His people and His saints; He will not let them return to folly.
Surely His salvation is near to those who fear Him, that His glory may dwell in our land.
Loving devotion and faithfulness have joined together; righteousness and {{shalom|peace}} have kissed.
Faithfulness sprouts from the earth, and righteousness looks down from heaven.
The LORD will indeed provide what is good, and our land will yield its increase.
{{tsedeq|Righteousness}} will go before Him to prepare the way for His steps.

chayah.gloss: To live, to be revived, to be brought back to life. The psalmist asks God to breathe fresh life into a people who once knew forgiveness but now need renewal again.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant love and loyal devotion. The psalmist appeals to this quality of God as the ground for hoping in a fresh act of salvation.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, completeness. Here peace is personified as meeting and embracing righteousness, picturing the full reconciliation God's salvation brings.

tsedeq.gloss: Righteousness, rightness. Righteousness is pictured as a herald walking ahead of the LORD, preparing the way for His coming, language later applied to the forerunner of the Messiah.

basic.context: Sung by the sons of Korah, this psalm remembers a time when God forgave His people's sin and restored them, then turns to plead for that same mercy again. It closes with a beautiful picture of God's love, truth, righteousness, and peace all meeting together to bless the land.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes a specific season of national sin, judgment, and partial restoration, and it prescribes an abiding pattern: remember God's past grace, confess present need honestly, and wait for His word of peace rather than manufacturing your own relief.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalmist asks God to chayah, revive or bring back to life, His discouraged people, verse 6. He appeals to chesed, God's steadfast covenant love, verse 7. And in the psalm's climax, shalom, peace, meets tsedeq, righteousness, in a kiss, verse 10, while righteousness itself goes before the LORD to prepare His way, verse 13.
basic.typology: The meeting of loving devotion and faithfulness, righteousness and peace, in verse 10 pictures what happens fully at the cross, where God's justice and mercy embrace. And righteousness going before the LORD to prepare His way, verse 13, anticipates the forerunner who prepares the way of the Lord before Christ's coming.
basic.christ: At the cross, God's righteousness and peace truly kiss: His justice against sin and His mercy toward sinners meet in the death and resurrection of Jesus, so that He is both just and the justifier of the one who has faith, Romans 3:25-26. Christ Himself is our peace, Ephesians 2:14, the answer to this psalm's longing for revival and reconciliation.
basic.god: God is shown as one who has forgiven and restrained His wrath before, and who can be trusted to do so again. He does not hold His anger forever; He listens, speaks peace, and provides what is good, while never asking His people to abandon righteousness to receive it.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3:25-26 shows God's righteousness and mercy meeting in Christ's atoning death. Isaiah 40:3 and Matthew 3:3 show righteousness, in the person of a forerunner, preparing the way of the Lord. Ephesians 2:14 names Christ Himself as our peace.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's plea for revival and its vision of love, truth, righteousness, and peace meeting together find their answer in Christ, in whom God's justice and mercy kiss at the cross, and who goes before His people to prepare the way into God's presence.

context.literary: A communal lament and petition of the sons of Korah, moving from remembered grace, verses 1-3, through present plea, verses 4-7, to a listening pause, verses 8-9, and a closing vision of restored blessing, verses 10-13.
context.historical: Most likely composed after a partial national restoration, such as the early return from Babylonian exile, when the people had tasted God's forgiveness yet still awaited the fuller revival of the land and their fortunes.
context.canonical: Standing near the end of Book III of the Psalter, this psalm's honest tension between remembered grace and present longing anticipates the deeper crisis voiced in Psalm 89, which closes the book on a note of unresolved hope in God's covenant promises.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground a present plea for national revival in the remembered pattern of God's past forgiveness, and to voice confident hope that God's love, truth, righteousness, and peace will together bring lasting restoration.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a particular community's experience of past mercy and present need; it prescribes remembering God's grace as fuel for present prayer, and waiting to hear God speak rather than presuming upon Him.

debates.question: Does verse 1, 'You restored Jacob from captivity,' describe the return from Babylonian exile or an earlier national deliverance?

views.label: It refers to the post-exilic return under Cyrus
views.case: The psalm's tension between a past restoration and a present, still-unfinished renewal fits the early post-exilic years, when the people had returned to the land but still awaited full blessing, similar to the setting of Haggai and Nehemiah.

views.label: It refers to an earlier deliverance, such as relief from a military threat
views.case: The language of forgiveness and restrained wrath could as easily describe any occasion when God relented from judgment on Israel, without requiring a specific post-exilic setting.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm's pattern holds: God's proven past mercy becomes the very ground for present prayer, and that pattern reaches its fullest expression in the finished, once-for-all mercy shown in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The psalmist pleads that God would chayah, revive, His people again, verse 6, appealing to His chesed, steadfast love, verse 7. The psalm's climax pictures shalom, peace, meeting tsedeq, righteousness, in a kiss, verse 10, and righteousness itself walking ahead of the LORD to prepare His way, verse 13.

typology.type: Loving devotion and faithfulness meeting, righteousness and peace kissing, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled at the cross, where God's justice against sin and His mercy toward sinners meet fully in Christ, Romans 3:25-26, Romans 5:1.

typology.type: Righteousness going before Him to prepare the way, verse 13
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the forerunner who prepares the way of the Lord, Isaiah 40:3, fulfilled in John the Baptist before Christ, Matthew 3:3.

study.christ: The psalm's vision of righteousness and peace kissing, verse 10, is fulfilled at the cross, where God shows Himself both just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus, Romans 3:25-26. Christ Himself, who reconciles sinners to God and to one another, is called our peace, Ephesians 2:14, the true and lasting answer to this psalm's cry, 'Revive us again.'
study.god: God is shown to have forgiven and restrained His wrath before, and to be a God who listens and speaks peace rather than remaining silent. He does not sustain anger forever, and He provides what is good for the land, while righteousness and truth remain central to how He restores His people.

crossReferences.note: God's righteousness and mercy meet fully in the atoning death of Christ, echoing this psalm's vision of righteousness and peace kissing.
Righteousness or a voice preparing the way of the Lord, fulfilled in John the Baptist before Christ.
Christ Himself is our peace, the reconciliation this psalm longs for.

study.oneStory: This psalm remembers God's past forgiveness and pleads for a fresh revival, closing with a vision of steadfast love, faithfulness, righteousness, and peace meeting together. That vision comes fully true in Christ, in whom God's justice and mercy embrace at the cross, and who goes before His people to prepare the way home to God.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist remember God doing for His people in verses 1-3?

options.text: He forgave their iniquity, covered their sin, and restrained His fury.
options.note: The opening verses recall a specific, past act of God's forgiveness and restraint of wrath.

options.text: He punished them without any relief.
options.note: The text describes forgiveness and restrained anger, not unrelieved punishment.

options.text: He restored Jacob from captivity.
options.note: This is true and part of what is remembered, verse 1, though the fuller emphasis in verses 2-3 is on forgiveness and restrained wrath.

options.text: He sent them into exile for the first time.
options.note: The psalm remembers restoration from captivity, not the beginning of exile.

quiz.q: What is the psalmist's central plea in verses 4-7?

options.text: That God would revive His people again and show them His loving devotion.
options.note: Verses 6-7 make the plea explicit: revive us again, show us Your loving devotion.

options.text: That God would give them more land.
options.note: The plea is for revival and renewed favor, not territorial expansion.

options.text: That God would punish their enemies immediately.
options.note: The psalm's focus is inward, pleading for the people's own revival, not judgment on enemies.

options.text: That God would put away His displeasure toward them.
options.note: This is a true part of the plea, verse 4, though the fuller request also includes revival, verse 6, and loving devotion, verse 7.

quiz.q: According to verse 8, what does the psalmist resolve to do?

options.text: Listen to what God the LORD will say, since He will speak peace to His people.
options.note: Verse 8 pictures the psalmist pausing to listen for God's word of peace.

options.text: Speak first and demand an answer from God.
options.note: The psalmist resolves to listen, not to demand.

options.text: Stop praying altogether.
options.note: The psalm continues in confident prayer, not silence.

options.text: Wait for God's salvation, since it is near to those who fear Him.
options.note: This truth appears in verse 9, closely related, though the specific resolve named in verse 8 is to listen for what God will say.

quiz.q: What beautiful picture appears in verse 10?

options.text: Loving devotion and faithfulness joining together; righteousness and peace kissing.
options.note: This is the psalm's climactic image of reconciled attributes meeting in blessing.

options.text: Two armies meeting in battle.
options.note: The image is one of reconciliation and embrace, not conflict.

options.text: The sun and moon standing still.
options.note: That image belongs elsewhere in Scripture, not this verse.

options.text: Faithfulness sprouting from the earth and righteousness looking down from heaven.
options.note: This closely related picture appears in verse 11, but verse 10 specifically pictures loving devotion, faithfulness, righteousness, and peace meeting and kissing.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's vision of righteousness and peace meeting point to Christ?

options.text: It is fulfilled at the cross, where God's justice and mercy meet fully in Jesus.
options.note: Romans 3:25-26 shows God as both just and the justifier through Christ's atoning death.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament.
options.note: The psalm's language is directly echoed in Paul's description of the cross.

options.text: It predicts the exact rebuilding date of the Jerusalem temple.
options.note: The psalm's vision is about reconciliation, not a construction timeline.

options.text: It anticipates a future, final peace for all creation.
options.note: This captures a true future dimension of the psalm's hope, but understates that its central fulfillment is already secured in Christ's finished work at the cross.

questions: The psalmist remembers God's past forgiveness before pleading for present revival. What past evidence of God's grace in your own life can fuel your prayers today?
Verse 6 asks, 'Will You not revive us again?' Where do you most need God to breathe fresh life into a tired or discouraged part of your faith?
The psalmist resolves to listen for what God will say rather than rushing ahead. What would it look like this week to wait and listen for God's word of peace instead of forcing your own solution?
Righteousness and peace kiss in verse 10, a picture fulfilled at the cross. How does knowing that God's justice and mercy have already met in Christ change how you come to Him with your own sin?
Righteousness goes before the LORD to prepare His way in verse 13. Where is God asking you to prepare the way for His work, in your own heart or in the lives of others?

translationNotes.note: Restored Jacob from captivity can also be rendered restored the fortunes of Jacob, a broader phrase that need not refer only to literal exile.
Kissed pictures loving devotion, faithfulness, righteousness, and peace as personified figures embracing, a vivid image of full reconciliation.
Righteousness will go before Him pictures righteousness as a herald preparing the way for a king's arrival, language echoed in the ministry of John the Baptist.

meta.notes: BSB text obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB (Berean Standard Bible), cross-checked against the section map bounds (Psalm 85:1-13, 13 verses). No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) was confirmed this session specifically on Psalm 85, so status is none.
